Other Formats

  • PDF

Effect of Weather, Day of Season, and Registration Status on Bike Rentals

STAT 3302: Statistical Modeling for Discovery II

Authors

Colin Farrell

Paarth Mahajan

Aayush Sharma

Tony Zheng

Published

April 22, 2026

Abstract

Introduction

Bike rentals vary from day-to-day depending on various weather, temporal, and user conditions. By analyzing which conditions have the greatest effect on the estimated number of rentals, we can find what conditions have an impact or don’t, and how these impacts interact with each other. As such, we ask the following questions:

(RQ.1)

Does the fact that it is a workday (not a weekend/holiday) affect the number of Bike Rentals?

(RQ.2)

Do weather conditions affect the number of Bike Rentals among registered users vs. casual users?

(RQ.3)

Does the fact that it is a workday (or not) affect the number of Bike Rentals among registered users vs. casual users differently?

(RQ.4)

Do the number of rentals change through a season (e.g. do rentals taper off as a season progresses)?

These questions address individual bike rental conditions and the way user they interact between each other (if one condition occurring cancels out another or strengthens its effect).

Data

The Bike Sharing dataset was sourced from (Fanaee-T 2013). It contains the daily and hourly counts of bike rentals in the Capital bike-share system in 2011 and 2012, augmented with weather and temporal information.

We use the hourly dataset; it contains a total of 17379 counts.

Predictors

Dataset predictors we used are described below

Predictor Description
temp The normalized temperature of the hour (unitless), in \(\left[0, 1\right]\). Found by taking the temperature in \(C^{\circ}\) and dividing by 41
windspeed The normalized windspeed of a day (unitless), in \(\left[0, 1\right]\). Found by taking the windspeed of the hour (unit is unstated in dataset) and dividing by 41
hum The normalized humidity (unitless), in \(\left[0, 1\right]\); taken from original humidity and then dividing by 100
weathersit Factor representing the amount of precipitation/cloud cover. In \(\{1, 2, 3, 4\}\), where
1: Clear, Few clouds, Partly cloudy, Partly cloudy
2: Mist + Cloudy, Mist + Broken clouds, Mist + Few clouds, Mist
3: Light Snow, Light Rain + Thunderstorm + Scattered clouds, Light Rain + Scattered clouds
4: Heavy Rain + Ice Pallets + Thunderstorm + Mist, Snow + Fog
season Indicator for what season it is. In the data, is an integer from 1-4, where 1 is Spring, 2 Summer, 3 Fall, and 4 Winter. For our model, we transform it into categorical data where Spr is for Spring, Sum is for Summer, Aut is for Fall, and Win is for Winter
workingday Indicator of if day was a workday or not (1 if workday, 0 if not)

Created Predictors

We also create new predictors from the dataset, described below

Predictor Description
user_type Indicator of if the number of rentals is taken from the casual or registered columns of the dataset (0 if casual, 1 if registered). We reshaped the data to be longer: instead of each row containing both a casual and registered count, we separated them out into two rows, with the casual row having user_type = 0, and 1 for the registered row
Day of season The day of the season (e.g. 0 for the first day of Spring, 1 for the second, etc…, then 0 for the first day of Fall, 1 for the second, etc…).

Our modified dataset then contains 34758 counts (i.e. double that of the original, since each row gets split into two).

Exploratory Plots

We look at exploratory plots relevant to each of our research questions

By workday

We can look at how RQ.1 and RQ.3 might be answered by plotting workingday against the rental counts, faceted by both user_type and workingday. We plot the proportion of counts accounted for by each bin of rentals in each group to see how the distribution itself changes, without it being affected by the total amount of counts in each group.

It seems that we generally see a significant change in rentals on working days, but this association is reversed for registered users. Casual users tend to see higher rental counts on non-working days, but registered users tend to see higher rental counts on working days. This suggests that the answer to RQ.3 might be that registered and casual users are affected by working days differently.

By user_type

To explore RQ.2 We can first explore how bike rentals vary by user_type.

It seems that the distribution of registered rentals is skewed higher than for casual users: casual counts are higher when the # of rentals is less than about 100, but registered counts are higher for rentals greater than that. In other words, it seems that user_type has an impact on the total number of rentals.

By temp

We can see that registered users generally have a higher baseline usage from casual users, even when comparing rentals for registered users at lower temperatures to casual users at higher temperatures.

We can also see that there seems to be a loosely positive association between temperature and the number of rentals; as the temperature increased, the number of rentals seemed to increase. However, it doesn’t seem that there is a large difference between registered and casual users in the size of this association (i.e. the amount of rentals increases about the same per temperature increase between casual and registered users).

By day_of_season

We can explore RQ.4 by plotting the day_of_season against the rental counts.

It doesn’t seem that there’s a strong relationship here; regardless of how far a season is along, it seems that the distribution of rentals stays relatively constant.

Methods

Model

We fit model \[ Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \]

with link function \[ \log(\lambda_i) = X_i^T\beta \]

for \(i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}\).

\(Y_i\) is the observed number of bike rentals for the hour of the \(i\)-th observation. For each hour, there are two \(Y\):

  • One for casual users
  • One for registered users

Then, \(X_i \in \mathbb{R}^{(p+1) \times n}\) and \(\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{p + 1}\), where - \(n\) is the number of samples in the hour dataset times two (one for each registered count, one for casual) - \(p\) is the total number of predictors we’re including, with intercept term \(\beta_0 = \beta_{0, i}\) for \(\forall i\) - \(X_{i, 1} = 1\) for \(\forall i\) - and all other \(X_{i, j}\) correspond to the value \(j\)-th predictor for observation \(i\) \(i \in {1, 2, \dots, n}\).

Predictors

Our model includes all those described in Predictors, along with an interaction between userType and each of those predictors. This is done to see how registered users are affected by each other predictor compared to casual users.

We also use stepAIC (bidirectional stepwise model selection using AIC) to determine if our model would have a better fit by ablating any predictors.

Results

We get fitted model:


Call:
glm(formula = rentals ~ (temp + windspeed + hum + weathersit + 
    workingday + day_of_season + season) * user_type, family = poisson(), 
    data = bike_transformed)

Coefficients:
                                    Estimate Std. Error  z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)                        2.906e+00  8.519e-03  341.176  < 2e-16 ***
temp                               3.621e+00  1.125e-02  321.782  < 2e-16 ***
windspeed                          3.365e-01  1.097e-02   30.674  < 2e-16 ***
hum                               -2.105e+00  8.145e-03 -258.460  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit2                        1.345e-01  3.258e-03   41.295  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit3                       -1.025e-01  7.111e-03  -14.407  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit4                       -3.154e-01  3.536e-01   -0.892   0.3724    
workingday                        -8.667e-01  2.547e-03 -340.287  < 2e-16 ***
day_of_season                      1.526e-03  5.026e-05   30.366  < 2e-16 ***
seasonSum                          3.534e-01  5.628e-03   62.805  < 2e-16 ***
seasonAut                         -4.757e-02  6.565e-03   -7.246 4.28e-13 ***
seasonWin                          5.619e-01  5.233e-03  107.378  < 2e-16 ***
user_typeregistered                1.416e+00  9.352e-03  151.439  < 2e-16 ***
temp:user_typeregistered          -1.714e+00  1.239e-02 -138.311  < 2e-16 ***
windspeed:user_typeregistered     -5.115e-02  1.218e-02   -4.201 2.66e-05 ***
hum:user_typeregistered            8.173e-01  8.988e-03   90.930  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit2:user_typeregistered   -5.280e-02  3.591e-03  -14.704  < 2e-16 ***
weathersit3:user_typeregistered   -5.399e-02  7.712e-03   -7.001 2.55e-12 ***
weathersit4:user_typeregistered    7.505e-01  3.601e-01    2.084   0.0372 *  
workingday:user_typeregistered     1.139e+00  2.909e-03  391.404  < 2e-16 ***
day_of_season:user_typeregistered  8.852e-04  5.552e-05   15.943  < 2e-16 ***
seasonSum:user_typeregistered     -2.916e-01  6.119e-03  -47.652  < 2e-16 ***
seasonAut:user_typeregistered     -1.608e-02  7.161e-03   -2.246   0.0247 *  
seasonWin:user_typeregistered     -1.199e-01  5.640e-03  -21.260  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 4643075  on 34757  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 2177102  on 34734  degrees of freedom
AIC: 2358946

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5

Which is the same after running stepAIC (backwards + forwards stepwise AIC model selection) starting from the full model and from the null model.

Diagnostics

We can generate a diagnostic plot for the estimated log rentals against residuals (for both Pearson and deviance residuals)

It appears that our model’s residuals are fairly well-fitted for low to medium amounts of bike rentals (0 to about 200). They seem to be centered around 0 for both Pearson and deviance.

For larger values, it appears that both types of residual are biased negatively, which means that our model is sometimes overestimating the number of rentals. This means that our model might not be appropriate; possible causes might be that there is some factor that causes bike rentals to be lower in these cases, or that our predictors are not truly linear (e.g. it might be that a more accurate relationship would be that \(\log(\mathbb{E}(rentals))\) is proportional to the log of temperature, rather than the actual temperature).

Discussion

(RQ.1)

Workdays also had an effect on the average number of rides, as the coefficient is statistically significant with associated p-value

[1] 0

and the model is statistically significant compared to the model without both workday coefficients, having p-value on the two predictors

[1] 0

The coefficient value for the workingday predictor is

workingday 
-0.8666524

Therefore, the estimated multiplicative change in mean number of riders when it is a working day compared to a non-working day, i.e., a holiday or weekend, is given by

[1] 0.4203564

which means that approximately 58% less average rides are estimated on a workday compared to a non-workday.

(RQ.2)

Does Weather affect casual users differently than registered users?

Coefficient summary for all weather conditions:

                                   Estimate      Pr(>|z|)
temp                             3.62107496  0.000000e+00
windspeed                        0.33645858 1.283160e-206
hum                             -2.10528547  0.000000e+00
weathersit2                      0.13452269  0.000000e+00
weathersit3                     -0.10245495  4.652071e-47
weathersit4                     -0.31541842  3.723710e-01
temp:user_typeregistered        -1.71388807  0.000000e+00
windspeed:user_typeregistered   -0.05114854  2.663021e-05
hum:user_typeregistered          0.81732178  0.000000e+00
weathersit2:user_typeregistered -0.05279786  6.048806e-49
weathersit3:user_typeregistered -0.05398828  2.549040e-12
weathersit4:user_typeregistered  0.75046453  3.716471e-02

Weather conditions affect both groups of users, but casual users are substantially more sensitive to weather than registered users. For example, the estimated mean rentals registered users are less affected by the temperature: the coefficient for temperature (i.e. the factor by which the estimated log mean number of rentals increases for unit of normalized temperature) for casual users is 3.62 vs. (3.62 + -1.71 = 1.91) for registered users.

The same goes for humidity, with estimated casual humidity coefficient -2.11 vs. registered humidity coefficient (-2.11 + 0.82 = -1.29).

For windspeed, the estimated casual coefficient was 0.34 vs. registered windspeedidity coefficient (0.34 + -0.05 = 0.29).

Finally, for precipitation, the casual coefficients were

weathersit2 weathersit3 weathersit4 
  0.1345227  -0.1024549  -0.3154184

versus

named numeric(0)

All of temp, hum, and windspeed (and their respective interaction terms with user_type) were statistically significant, with low p-values, which means that these observed relationships were significant. This was also true for each of the seasons and their interactions terms, except for the baseline coefficient for heavy precipitation (though its interaction term was significant). This indicates that there was not a statistically significant difference in bike rentals among casual users between a heavy precipitation versus. clear day, though this could be due to high collinearity between such conditions and other weather conditions (like windspeed and humidity).

Overall, causal users had strong sensitivity to temperature, humidity, and windspeed. Registered users are still affected by weather, but seems to be less affected, with the absolute value of each of their coefficients being smaller than the absolute value for casual users. Such a case is possibly due to more consistent baseline usage and behavior driven by necessity rather than comfort.

(RQ.3)

We want to know if workday status affects the bike rentals of casual and registered users differently.

\[ log(\lambda_i) = \cdots + \beta_{\text{wd}} \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{wd}] + \beta_{\text{wd:utr}} \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{wd}] \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{utr}] + \beta_{utr} \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{utr}] \]

The estimates for this interaction effect coefficient during model fit:

                                 Estimate  Std. Error Pr(>|z|)
workingday                     -0.8666524 0.002546827        0
workingday:user_typeregistered  1.1386038 0.002909022        0
user_typeregistered             1.4163179 0.009352376        0
                                 Estimate  Std. Error Pr(>|z|)
workingday                     -0.8666524 0.002546827        0
workingday:user_typeregistered  1.1386038 0.002909022        0
user_typeregistered             1.4163179 0.009352376        0
Casual Multiplicative Difference in Rentals for Workingday:    0.42 
Registered Multiplicative Difference in Rentals for Workingday: 1.313

\[ \frac{\lambda_{wd,cas}}{\lambda_{nw,cas}} = exp\{ \beta_{wd}\} = 0.42 \]

\[ \frac{\lambda_{wd,reg}}{\lambda_{nw,reg}} = exp\{ \beta_{wd} + \beta_{wd:utr} \} = 1.313 \]

Analysis of Deviance Table

Model 1: rentals ~ (temp + windspeed + hum + weathersit + day_of_season + 
    season) * user_type + workingday + user_type
Model 2: rentals ~ (temp + windspeed + hum + weathersit + workingday + 
    day_of_season + season) * user_type
  Resid. Df Resid. Dev Df Deviance  Pr(>Chi)    
1     34735    2328658                          
2     34734    2177102  1   151556 < 2.2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(RQ.4)

Our estimated day_of_season coefficients were statistically significant, since day_of_season and day_of_season:user_typeregistered had individual p-values of

                    day_of_season day_of_season:user_typeregistered 
                    1.563248e-202                      3.162649e-57

and a p-value when added together

[1] 0

with individual values

                    day_of_season day_of_season:user_typeregistered 
                     0.0015263168                      0.0008852259

These indicate that there was a positive relationship between the day of the season and average bike rentals. Under this, bike rentals increased in later parts of seasons for all users - though to a slightly greater extent for registered users. Specifically, it is estimated that for every day that passed in a season, the estimated mean number of casual bike rentals in an hour increased by a factor of

[1] 1.001527

and for registered users

[1] 1.002414

However, these values are very small, especially since this effect prorated over an entire season (about 90 days) would only result in a multiplicative increase in bike rentals by

[1] 1.242392

which is a fairly small increase. Therefore, even though the coefficient is statistically significant, it would seem that there isn’t a practically significant effect on rentals, with weather and user_type having a much greater effect on the number of rentals.

Conclusion

Bike rentals are significantly associated with different climate/weather conditions. They

  • Significantly increased for higher temperatures
  • Increased for non-working days
  • Increased as a season went along (later parts of a season were associated with more rentals)

References

Fanaee-T, Hadi. 2013. “Bike Sharing.” UCI Machine Learning Repository.

Appendix

The model was fit using

model = glm(rentals ~ (temp + windspeed + hum + weathersit + workingday + day_of_season + season)*user_type, bike_transformed, family = poisson())

s = summary(model)
s
